title: Excess nitrogen from croplands
subtitle: "Excess nitrogen" is the difference between nutrient inputs (from fertilizers, manure, and fixation from legumes) and the amount harvested in crop material. This represents nitrogen that are lost to the environment and can create ecological imbalances on ecosystems and in water bodies.
caption:
Excess nitrogen from croplands, 2009
"Excess nitrogen" is the difference between nutrient inputs (from fertilizers, manure, and fixation from legumes) and the amount harvested in crop material. This represents nitrogen that are lost to the environment and can create ecological imbalances on ecosystems and in water bodies.

The map displays the total mass of excess nitrogen from croplands for the year 2009, measured in tonnes, with a color scale ranging from light yellow for low amounts to dark brown for the highest concentrations. The data reveals a highly uneven global distribution where specific regions account for the vast majority of nutrient surplus. The darkest brown shading, representing values exceeding 10 million tonnes, is concentrated in a few distinct areas: China, India, the United States, and Brazil. These four nations stand out as the primary sources of excess nitrogen on the map. Surrounding these major contributors, countries in Western Europe, including France and Germany, along with parts of Southeast Asia like Indonesia, fall into the orange bands, indicating surplus levels between 1 million and 10 million tonnes. In contrast, large portions of the globe show significantly lower figures. Most of sub-Saharan Africa, including nations like Niger and Chad, along with Russia and Canada, appear in light yellow or pale orange, corresponding to values below 250,000 tonnes or in the lower ranges of the scale. Australia and Mexico also sit in the intermediate orange bands. The visual pattern shows that the highest nitrogen surpluses coincide with regions of intensive agricultural activity, while vast land areas in the northern latitudes and arid zones contribute minimal excess nutrients relative to the global leaders.
